cases.chiefComplaint: Productive cough and fever for 3 days
cases.history: Patient reports progressive worsening of cough with yellow-green sputum. Fever up to 39.2°C. Recent upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. No recent travel. Non-smoker. No chronic conditions.
cases.physicalExam: Alert and oriented. Respiratory distress mild. Crackles heard in right lower lung base. No wheezing. Tachypneic at rest.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe infiltrate with air bronchograms
supportingEvidence: Crackles localized to right lower lobe on auscultation
Elevated WBC count (14,200/μL) suggests bacterial process
Chest X-ray shows infiltrate with air bronchograms (diagnostic for pneumonia)
Elevated CRP (85 mg/L) confirms significant inflammation

supporting: Crackles in right lower lobe (focal finding)
challenging: Recent URI 2 weeks ago (could suggest viral progression)
No significant comorbidities (bronchitis often in healthy adults)

cases.clinicalReasoning: The combination of fever, productive cough, focal crackles, elevated inflammatory markers, and chest X-ray infiltrate meets criteria for community-acquired pneumonia. The infiltrate on imaging is the key discriminator from acute bronchitis, which typically has normal chest X-ray.
cases.symptomAnalysis: The patient's symptom constellation strongly suggests bacterial pneumonia. The productive cough with purulent sputum, high fever, and focal findings on exam are classic. The X-ray infiltrate confirms the diagnosis.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Chest X-ray infiltrate (pneumonia) vs. normal X-ray (bronchitis)

cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent cough for 10 days
cases.history: Patient developed cough after a cold 10 days ago. Initially dry, now with some clear sputum. Low-grade fever (37.8°C) for first 3 days, now resolved. No shortness of breath. Mild chest discomfort with coughing. No recent antibiotic use.
cases.physicalExam: Alert, comfortable. No respiratory distress. Bilateral expiratory wheezes throughout lung fields. No focal crackles. No dullness to percussion.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray shows no infiltrate, no consolidation. Mild bronchial wall thickening.
supportingEvidence: Productive cough lasting >7 days (can indicate pneumonia)
History of fever in first 3 days (bacterial infection possible)
Chest discomfort reported (pleuritic pain in pneumonia)
Wheezing on exam (can occur in pneumonia with bronchospasm)
Recent viral URI (post-viral bacterial superinfection is common)

challenging: No focal crackles (pneumonia usually has focal findings)
Normal WBC count (bacterial pneumonia typically elevated)
Chest X-ray shows NO infiltrate (rules out pneumonia)

cases.clinicalReasoning: This presentation is consistent with acute bronchitis following a viral upper respiratory infection. The absence of infiltrate on chest X-ray, normal inflammatory markers, and lack of focal findings make pneumonia unlikely. The wheezing suggests bronchial inflammation rather than consolidation.
cases.symptomAnalysis: The prolonged cough after viral URI is classic for acute bronchitis. The key is that inflammatory markers are normal and chest X-ray is clear, ruling out pneumonia.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Normal chest X-ray (bronchitis) vs. infiltrate (pneumonia)

cases.chiefComplaint: Severe chest pain radiating to left arm for 2 hours
cases.history: Patient reports sudden onset of crushing substernal chest pain while at rest. Pain radiates to left arm and jaw. Associated with diaphoresis, nausea. No relief with rest. History of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Smoker (20 pack-years). Family history of early MI (father at age 52).
cases.physicalExam: Appears anxious, diaphoretic. No JVD. Heart sounds: S4 gallop present. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema.
cases.imaging: ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF. Q waves not yet developed.
supportingEvidence: Crushing substernal chest pain with radiation (classic MI presentation)
Troponin I significantly elevated at 2.8 ng/mL (confirms myocardial injury)
ST-segment elevation on ECG in inferior leads (diagnostic for STEMI)
Multiple cardiac risk factors: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, family history
Pain at rest (not exertion-related) for >30 minutes

challenging: No prior angina history (but MI can be first presentation)

cases.clinicalReasoning: This is a classic STEMI presentation. The combination of typical chest pain, ST elevation on ECG, and elevated troponin confirms acute myocardial infarction. The inferior ST elevations (II, III, aVF) indicate right coronary artery occlusion. Immediate reperfusion therapy (PCI or thrombolytics) is indicated.
cases.symptomAnalysis: The patient meets all criteria for STEMI: chest pain >20 minutes, ST elevation >1mm in contiguous leads, and elevated cardiac biomarkers. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate catheterization.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Troponin elevation + ST elevation (STEMI) vs. normal troponin (Unstable Angina)

cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath over 3 days
cases.history: Patient with known COPD presents with worsening dyspnea, increased sputum production (green), and chest tightness. Using rescue inhaler more frequently with minimal relief. No fever. Recent viral illness 1 week ago.
cases.physicalExam: Mild respiratory distress. Using accessory muscles. Diffuse expiratory wheezes and prolonged expiration. Barrel-shaped chest. Distant breath sounds bilaterally.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragm, no infiltrate
cases.clinicalReasoning: Typical COPD exacerbation triggered by viral infection. Characterized by increased dyspnea, increased sputum, and increased sputum purulence.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Known COPD history + diffuse wheezes + hyperinflation on X-ray

cases.chiefComplaint: Bilateral leg swelling and shortness of breath for 1 week
cases.history: Progressive dyspnea on exertion, now occurring with minimal activity. Orthopnea (sleeps on 3 pillows). Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Lower extremity edema. History of hypertension and prior MI 3 years ago.
cases.physicalExam: JVD present. Bilateral crackles in lung bases. S3 gallop. 3+ pitting edema in lower extremities bilaterally.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions, Kerley B lines
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic heart failure exacerbation with volume overload. Elevated BNP confirms diagnosis. Bilateral crackles, JVD, and peripheral edema indicate systemic congestion.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Elevated BNP + cardiomegaly + volume overload signs

cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset shortness of breath and chest pain
cases.history: Patient developed sudden dyspnea and sharp chest pain 2 hours ago. Pain worsens with deep breath. Recent long-haul flight from Moscow (12 hours). Taking oral contraceptives. No prior cardiopulmonary disease.
cases.physicalExam: Tachypneic, anxious. Clear lung sounds bilaterally. No leg swelling. Heart sounds regular, no murmurs.
cases.imaging: CT pulmonary angiography: filling defect in right pulmonary artery
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic PE presentation with Wells criteria positive factors: recent immobilization (flight), oral contraceptives, tachycardia, pleuritic pain.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Recent long flight + elevated D-dimer + CT angiography confirms PE

cases.chiefComplaint: Exertional chest pressure for 2 weeks
cases.history: Patient reports substernal pressure with walking uphill, relieved by rest within 5 minutes. No pain at rest. History of diabetes and hypertension. Smoker (30 pack-years).
cases.physicalExam: Comfortable at rest. Heart sounds regular. Lungs clear. No edema.
cases.imaging: ECG at rest: normal sinus rhythm, no ST changes
cases.clinicalReasoning: Predictable exertional chest pain relieved by rest, multiple cardiac risk factors, normal troponin.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Exertion-related + relieved by rest + normal troponin

cases.chiefComplaint: Barking cough and difficulty breathing for 1 day
cases.history: Child developed hoarse voice and seal-like barking cough overnight. Stridor audible. Mild fever. Recent cold symptoms for 2 days.
cases.physicalExam: Alert but distressed. Inspiratory stridor. Mild retractions. Barking cough noted.
cases.imaging: Neck X-ray: steeple sign (subglottic narrowing)
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic croup: barking cough, stridor, steeple sign on X-ray. Viral etiology most common.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Barking cough + stridor + steeple sign

cases.chiefComplaint: Palpitations and lightheadedness for 3 hours
cases.history: Patient woke with rapid heartbeat, feels dizzy. No chest pain. Drinks 5-6 cups of coffee daily. No cardiac history.
cases.physicalExam: Alert, anxious. Irregularly irregular pulse. Lungs clear. No murmurs.
cases.imaging: ECG: irregularly irregular rhythm, no P waves, narrow QRS complexes
cases.clinicalReasoning: Irregularly irregular pulse with absent P waves on ECG diagnostic for atrial fibrillation.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Irregularly irregular rhythm + no P waves on ECG

cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe chest pain and shortness of breath
cases.history: Patient developed sudden stabbing chest pain while coughing. Pain is pleuritic. Recent rib fracture from fall 2 weeks ago. Tall, thin build. Smoker.
cases.physicalExam: Respiratory distress. Decreased breath sounds on left. Hyperresonance to percussion on left.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: absence of lung markings in left hemithorax, collapsed left lung
supportingEvidence: Decreased breath sounds on affected side
Hyperresonance to percussion (air in pleural space)
Risk factors: tall thin build, smoking, recent trauma
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic spontaneous pneumothorax in a tall thin smoker. X-ray is diagnostic.
cases.symptomAnalysis: Sudden onset with pleuritic pain and unilateral findings classic for pneumothorax.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Decreased breath sounds + hyperresonance + X-ray showing collapsed lung

cases.chiefComplaint: Chest pain after emotional stress
cases.history: Patient developed chest pain during argument with spouse. Pain resolved spontaneously after 20 minutes. No prior cardiac history. Recent death of family member.
cases.physicalExam: Comfortable. Heart regular. Lungs clear. No focal findings.
cases.imaging: ECG: T-wave inversions in anterior leads. Echocardiography: apical ballooning, reduced EF 35%
supportingEvidence: Chest pain triggered by emotional stress
Mild troponin elevation without significant coronary disease
cases.clinicalReasoning: Takotsubo (broken heart syndrome) presents after acute stress with chest pain and apical ballooning. Distinguished from MI by echo findings and resolution.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would have regional wall motion abnormality, not apical ballooning
Would not have troponin elevation or echo changes

cases.symptomAnalysis: Stress-induced chest pain with characteristic apical ballooning differentiates this from ACS.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Apical ballooning on echo + emotional stress trigger

cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic cough with blood-tinged sputum for 6 weeks
cases.history: Progressive cough, occasional hemoptysis. 15 lb weight loss. Night sweats. Immigrated from high TB prevalence area 2 months ago. No HIV risk factors.
cases.physicalExam: Cachectic appearance. Decreased breath sounds right upper lobe. No acute distress.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: right upper lobe cavitary lesion. Sputum AFB smear: positive for acid-fast bacilli
supportingEvidence: Recent immigration from high TB prevalence area
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic TB presentation with positive AFB smear. Cavitary lesion in upper lobe is typical for pulmonary TB.
cases.symptomAnalysis: Chronic cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, and cavitary lesion form classic TB constellation.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Positive AFB smear + cavitary upper lobe lesion + epidemiology

cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp chest pain worsening when lying flat
cases.history: Patient developed sharp chest pain 2 days ago, worse with deep breath and lying flat, relieved by sitting forward. Recent viral illness (flu-like symptoms) 2 weeks ago.
cases.physicalExam: Sitting forward. Friction rub heard at left sternal border. No JVD. Lungs clear.
cases.imaging: ECG: diffuse ST elevation, PR depression. Echocardiography: no pericardial effusion
supportingEvidence: Chest pain with ST elevation on ECG (can suggest MI)
Young male with acute chest pain

challenging: Pain worse lying flat, better sitting forward (positional - classic for pericarditis, NOT MI)
Friction rub on exam (pathognomonic for pericarditis)

cases.clinicalReasoning: This is acute pericarditis, not MI. Key differentiators: positional pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, normal troponin. Recent viral infection is typical trigger.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Friction rub + positional pain + diffuse ST elevation
Would have regional ST elevation, elevated troponin
Would not have ST changes or friction rub

cases.symptomAnalysis: Friction rub and positional pain are pathognomonic for pericarditis. ST elevation is diffuse, not regional.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Friction rub + positional pain + diffuse ST/PR changes + normal troponin = Pericarditis, NOT MI

cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden difficulty breathing and wheezing while eating peanuts
cases.history: Child developed sudden respiratory distress while eating peanut butter sandwich. Progressive stridor and difficulty swallowing. No prior allergies known.
cases.physicalExam: Severe respiratory distress. Inspiratory and expiratory stridor. Drooling. Tripod position.
cases.imaging: Direct laryngoscopy: foreign body (peanut) lodged at vocal cords
supportingEvidence: No rash or urticaria (would be present in anaphylaxis)

challenging: Eating peanuts could suggest allergic reaction

cases.clinicalReasoning: Acute foreign body aspiration. Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction. Direct visualization confirms diagnosis.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would have rash, urticaria, no foreign body

cases.symptomAnalysis: Acute onset during eating with stridor and foreign body visualization is diagnostic.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Sudden onset while eating + foreign body visualized = Aspiration, not anaphylaxis

cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache and nosebleed
cases.history: Patient developed sudden severe headache and spontaneous nosebleed. No trauma. History of hypertension, non-compliant with medications. Recent stress.
cases.physicalExam: Anxious, diaphoretic. Fundoscopy: papilledema, hemorrhages. No focal neurological deficits.
cases.imaging: CT head: no hemorrhage. ECG: left ventricular hypertrophy
cases.clinicalReasoning: Hypertensive emergency requires BP >180/120 with end-organ damage. Here: papilledema and acute kidney injury present.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not have papilledema or BP 220/130

cases.symptomAnalysis: Severely elevated BP with papilledema and renal injury defines hypertensive emergency.
cases.keyDifferentiator: BP >180/120 + papilledema + acute kidney injury

cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath over months
cases.history: Gradual onset dyspnea on exertion over 6 months. Dry cough. Fatigue. Works as pigeon breeder for 20 years. Non-smoker.
cases.physicalExam: Bibasilar fine crackles (Velcro-like). Digital clubbing present. No wheezing.
cases.imaging: Chest HRCT: ground-glass opacities, reticular pattern. Pulmonary function tests: restrictive pattern
supportingEvidence: Positive serum precipitins for avian proteins
cases.clinicalReasoning: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis from bird antigen exposure. Positive precipitins and exposure history are diagnostic.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not have exposure history or positive precipitins

cases.symptomAnalysis: Occupation-related exposure with positive precipitins confirms hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Pigeon breeder exposure + positive avian precipitins

cases.chiefComplaint: Syncope during basketball game
cases.history: Patient collapsed during intense physical activity. Brief loss of consciousness. Family history: sudden death in brother at age 22 during exercise.
cases.physicalExam: Well-appearing. Harsh systolic murmur at left sternal border, decreases with squatting. Normal S1/S2.
cases.imaging: Echocardiography: asymmetric septal hypertrophy (18mm), systolic anterior motion of mitral valve, LV outflow obstruction
supportingEvidence: Family history of sudden death in young relative (genetic)
Harsh systolic murmur that decreases with squatting
SAM of mitral valve on echo

challenging: Single episode could suggest simple vasovagal

cases.clinicalReasoning: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. Echo findings and family history are diagnostic.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not have murmur or echo abnormalities
Would need Holter to confirm, echo findings point to HCM

cases.symptomAnalysis: Exertional syncope in young athlete with family history and echo findings is HCM until proven otherwise.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Exertional syncope + family history + septal hypertrophy >15mm

cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic productive cough for 3 months
cases.history: Daily productive cough with white sputum for >3 months. Occurring for 2 consecutive years. Smoker (40 pack-years). Morning cough worst.
cases.physicalExam: Wheezes and rhonchi bilaterally. Prolonged expiratory phase. No clubbing.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: increased bronchovascular markings, hyperinflation. Spirometry: FEV1/FVC <70%, bronchodilator responsive
cases.correctDiagnosis: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) - Chronic Bronchitis

challenging: Age >40 and smoking history (COPD more likely)
Productive cough >3 months per year for 2 years (DEFINES chronic bronchitis)

cases.clinicalReasoning: This is COPD (chronic bronchitis phenotype), not asthma. Defined by productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 years in a smoker. FEV1/FVC <70% confirms obstruction.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Smoking history + chronic productive cough definition
Asthma typically starts younger, lacks chronic sputum
Would need CT to confirm, less likely

cases.symptomAnalysis: Chronic bronchitis is defined by productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years. Smoking history and obstructive spirometry confirm COPD.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Smoking + productive cough ≥3 months × 2 years + FEV1/FVC <70% = COPD, not asthma

cases.chiefComplaint: Fainting spells when standing up
cases.history: Recurrent near-syncope and syncope when standing from seated position. Symptoms resolve when lying down. On multiple blood pressure medications.
cases.physicalExam: Alert. Heart regular. Lungs clear. No neurological deficits.
cases.imaging: ECG: normal sinus rhythm. Holter monitor: no arrhythmias
supportingEvidence: Syncope episodes (can be caused by arrhythmia)

challenging: Symptoms only when standing (positional - NOT arrhythmia)
BP drops 50mmHg systolic upon standing (DEFINES orthostatic hypotension)
Heart rate does NOT increase appropriately (autonomic dysfunction)

cases.clinicalReasoning: This is orthostatic hypotension, NOT arrhythmia. Diagnosis requires ≥20mmHg systolic drop (or ≥10 diastolic) within 3 minutes of standing. Here: 145→95 (50mmHg drop). Holter negative for arrhythmia.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not be positional, Holter would show arrhythmia

cases.symptomAnalysis: Positional syncope with documented BP drop >20mmHg upon standing is diagnostic for orthostatic hypotension. Likely medication-related in elderly patient.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Positional symptoms + BP drop 50mmHg standing + negative Holter = Orthostatic hypotension, NOT arrhythmia

cases.chiefComplaint: Dry cough and shortness of breath for 2 months
cases.history: Progressive dyspnea on exertion. Dry cough. Fatigue. No fever. Recent onset rash on shins (tender red nodules). No sick contacts.
cases.physicalExam: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on palpation. Erythema nodosum on shins. No wheezes.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (stage I). PFTs: restrictive pattern
cases.clinicalReasoning: Löfgren syndrome (acute sarcoidosis): bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + erythema nodosum + arthralgia. Excellent prognosis.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not have erythema nodosum or elevated ACE
Would be asymmetric, not bilateral hilar

cases.symptomAnalysis: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with erythema nodosum is pathognomonic for Löfgren syndrome (acute sarcoidosis).
cases.keyDifferentiator: Bilateral hilar LAD + erythema nodosum + elevated ACE

cases.chiefComplaint: Severe tearing chest pain radiating to back
cases.history: Sudden onset severe tearing chest pain radiating between shoulder blades. History of uncontrolled hypertension. Marfan-like body habitus.
cases.physicalExam: Diaphoretic, severe distress. BP differential: right arm 180/100, left arm 140/85. Diastolic murmur at left sternal border.
cases.imaging: CT angiography chest: Stanford Type A aortic dissection with intimal flap, false lumen
supportingEvidence: Sudden tearing/ripping chest pain to back (classic)
CT angiography: intimal flap and false lumen (diagnostic)
cases.clinicalReasoning: Type A aortic dissection is a surgical emergency. BP differential and tearing pain to back are classic. CT angiography is diagnostic.

differentialComparison.differentiator: Would not have BP differential or tearing pain

cases.symptomAnalysis: Tearing pain to back with BP differential and aortic regurgitation murmur is aortic dissection until proven otherwise.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Tearing pain + BP differential + CT shows dissection

cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe headache - worst of life
cases.history: Patient developed sudden explosive headache during weightlifting. Describes it as 'worst headache of my life.' Brief loss of consciousness. Nausea and vomiting. Photophobia.
cases.physicalExam: Neck stiffness present. Alert but in severe pain. No focal neurological deficits. Fundoscopy: no papilledema.
cases.imaging: CT head non-contrast: hyperdense material in subarachnoid spaces, blood in basal cisterns
cases.clinicalReasoning: Classic thunderclap headache ('worst headache of life') during exertion. CT shows subarachnoid blood. Likely ruptured aneurysm.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Thunderclap headache + CT shows subarachnoid blood

cases.chiefComplaint: Shortness of breath and swollen legs for 1 month
cases.history: Progressive dyspnea, worse at night. Orthopnea (sleeps on 4 pillows). PND. Bilateral leg swelling. Recently treated for pneumonia 6 weeks ago. History of IV drug use.
cases.physicalExam: JVD elevated. Pansystolic murmur at left lower sternal border. Hepatomegaly. 3+ pitting edema.
cases.imaging: Echocardiography: vegetation on tricuspid valve, severe TR, dilated right heart
cases.clinicalReasoning: Right-sided endocarditis (tricuspid) in IV drug user. Fever, new murmur, and vegetation on echo. Modified Duke criteria positive.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Fever + new murmur + vegetation on echo + IVDU history

cases.chiefComplaint: Severe shortness of breath and wheezing for 2 hours
cases.history: Known asthmatic. Ran out of inhaler 3 days ago. Developed progressive dyspnea, now unable to speak full sentences. Used friend's albuterol with minimal relief.
cases.physicalExam: Severe respiratory distress. Sitting upright. Diffuse wheezing and decreased air movement. Use of accessory muscles. Diaphoretic.
cases.imaging: Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, no infiltrate. Peak flow: <40% predicted
cases.clinicalReasoning: Life-threatening asthma exacerbation. Unable to speak sentences, elevated pCO2 (respiratory failure), peak flow <40%. Requires ICU.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Known asthma + unable to speak + elevated pCO2 + peak flow <40%

cases.chiefComplaint: Dizziness and fatigue for 2 weeks
cases.history: Progressive fatigue and dizziness. Episodes of near-syncope. Palpitations. History of hypertension on beta-blockers.
cases.physicalExam: Alert. Bradycardia. Cannon A waves in JVP. Heart rate varies from 35-45 bpm.
cases.imaging: ECG: complete AV dissociation, P waves and QRS complexes independent, ventricular rate 38
cases.clinicalReasoning: Third-degree AV block with complete AV dissociation. Requires permanent pacemaker. Cannon A waves (atria contract against closed AV valves) are classic.
cases.keyDifferentiator: Bradycardia + cannon A waves + complete AV dissociation on ECG

notes.sensible_foils: Cases marked with isFoil=true are intentional AI errors (30% of intervention cases). The AI recommendation is a plausible but incorrect diagnosis (sensible foil) to test overreliance.
notes.progressive_reveal: CriticInterface reveals labs, imaging, and differential comparison progressively. OracleInterface shows all evidence immediately.
notes.case_distribution: 5 pre-test (no AI), 15 intervention (with AI, 4-5 are foils), 5 post-test (no AI)
notes.foil_placement: Foils should not be consecutive. Distribute across respiratory and cardiovascular categories.
